agent_retro.css_handling: CSS classes were added to app.css, dependency injection directive was added.
agent_retro.lesson: The blocking defect was the DownloadLogs method generating JavaScript string but not executing it. The fix leverages JSRuntime.InvokeVoidAsync with string interpolation using @(Html.Raw(variable)) syntax to properly inject the variable into the JS template literal.

codebase_learnings: Blazor uses JSRuntime.InvokeVoidAsync("eval", js) to execute JavaScript injected into the page, @(Html.Raw(variable)) prevents Razor literal interpolation from breaking the JS template literal

The ILogParser interface and LogParser implementation handle both timestamped logs [HH:mm:ss.fff] Message and plain text messages, yielding LogMessage records with Timestamp, Content, and LineNumber fields
All required CSS classes for the logs widget were added to app.css with proper styling for timestamps, content, and empty states
The @inject directive for ILogParser LogParser is now present in BuildDetail.razor page directives
summary: Complete fix for the blocking defect. DownloadLogs method now properly executes JavaScript via JSRuntime.InvokeVoidAsync("eval", jsScript). The iframe execution section was removed and replaced with native logs widget. All CSS classes (.build-log-container, .build-log-timestamp, .build-log-content, .build-log-empty, .build-log-cached, .build-log-actions) were added to app.css. The @inject ILogParser LogParser directive was added to page directives.
test_summary: Manual visual testing required - Navigate to /builds/{jobId} to verify logs widget renders with timestamps and action buttons (Refresh, Copy, Download). The Download button should now trigger a file download via JavaScript.
